Q: Used Target Test Prep's Quant lessons to close knowledge gaps.
Watched every Quant lesson in TTP and credited that as the main improvement driver.
Used TTP question bank, some GMAT Club questions, and many official practice tests.
overall: Prepared for about eight months and around 250 total hours.
Used official practice tests throughout and saw scores range from 625 up to 735 before a 695 official result.
Took Manhattan Prep and Target Test Prep courses, finding TTP more useful because it enforced consistent weekly study.
Found structured course order helpful after earlier scattered study with YouTube one day and random practice the next.
Used the course to recover confidence after a jet-lagged 615 practice test.
Planned a retake because official mocks suggested a higher ceiling, but weighed that against application work.

Q: Recommended taking an initial mock to establish baseline strengths and weaknesses.
Used official material, GMAT Club explanations, and official mocks as the core resources.
For DS, used an AD/BCE decision framework on paper.
V: For RC, recommended jotting a couple of points per paragraph to create a mental map and answer faster.
Tracked mistakes early and categorized them by concept, logic, careless error, or timing.
DI: Used the same mistake buckets for DI: concept, logic, careless, and timing.
For DS, used an AD/BCE decision framework on paper.
overall: Prepared for about three months while working full-time, usually two to three hours on weekdays and more on weekends.
Believed the first three to four weeks are where many test-takers go wrong by using too many resources and no clear plan.
Recommended a staged prep flow: foundation, timed practice plus weaknesses, then mocks plus decision-making.
Advised not chasing score in the first one to two weeks; instead learn question types and format.
Built improvement through a simple loop: attempt, understand why wrong, and apply the lesson in the next set.
Warned not to exhaust official mocks too quickly because repeated questions reduce their diagnostic value.

V: Distinguished casual reading skill from GMAT-specific reading skill.
For RC, focused on passage structure, argument flow, and transition signals rather than passive content absorption.
For hard CR, paused 15-20 seconds after the question to anticipate what the answer should accomplish.
Used pre-analysis to avoid deciding between two attractive answer choices by gut feeling.
Invested four to five minutes upfront to understand RC passages, reducing rereading later.
Took light RC notes focused on keywords, sentence connections, and a one-sentence paragraph summary.
Created abbreviations for technical terms and tracked their function rather than memorizing the term itself.
Stopped relying on more volume with flawed technique and rebuilt the Verbal approach systematically.
Avoided studying Verbal when mentally exhausted after work because poor tired practice distorted confidence.
overall: Improved Verbal from roughly V80-82 plateau to V87 while scoring 685 overall.
Initially used OG practice, then used e-GMAT and Scholaranium for a more structured Verbal approach.
Learned that quality technique with fewer questions beat high-volume practice with bad habits.
Identified recurring loopholes rather than assuming more practice alone would fix the plateau.
